extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a lab‑scale gantry crane inspired by harbour cranes at the Port of Antwerp. It physically moves small pucks that represent containers, using a hoist motor (a stepper motor) driven by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller together with other electromechanical components. The crane is accessed via a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that talks to the crane's controllers, a Control Application (Python) that commands movements, a Logging Application (Python) that records state, and a Human‑Machine Interface (Python front‑end) for operator interaction. The system can run without the digital twin, but its compact size, well‑defined kinematics, and ability to log detailed state make it an ideal research and education platform for digital‑twin studies in the logistics/port handling domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin offers multiple services: (1) Trajectory Generator Service – uses the crane's kinematic model to compute optimal movement trajectories that finish with zero residual swinging; (2) Simulation Service – provides a software simulation of the crane and its controllers to evaluate generated trajectories; (3) Continuous Validation Service – continuously compares measured executed trajectories with simulated ones, calculates distance metrics, checks against a configurable threshold, and notifies operators via the dashboard when thresholds are exceeded; (4) Logging Application – continuously streams the crane’s state into a TimescaleDB database for historical analysis; (5) Dashboard – a Grafana visualization that displays logged data, trajectory traces, and validation alerts; (6) MQTT Broker – mediates signalling and coordination among the services. The primary users are the operator (who monitors and controls the crane) and researchers (who conduct validation, fault‑detection, and optimisation experiments).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on open‑source tooling and containerisation: Python programs implement the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, and Human‑Machine Interface; Docker Compose orchestrates six containers (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT Broker) and launches them with a single command; TimescaleDB serves as the time‑series database storing all sensor and log data; Grafana provides the visual dashboard; an MQTT broker enables inter‑service communication; CAD models and a kinematic model constitute the analytical foundation; the complete source code and documentation are hosted publicly on GitHub, allowing reproducible deployment on standard personal computers.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system comprises a single digital‑twin instance that is decomposed into six containerized services (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, MQTT Broker). These containers cooperate via MQTT and share the TimescaleDB; the physical crane is a separate entity. No hierarchical or multiple twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin follows a micro‑service architecture orchestrated with Docker Compose. Python applications implement the Control Application, Logging Application, and HMI; services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) communicate through an MQTT broker. Persistent data is stored in a TimescaleDB database and visualised with a Grafana dashboard. The overall system is modelled with the C4 approach, and containers are launched together with a single Docker‑compose command, providing resource isolation and network management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Inter‑service integration relies on the MQTT protocol for messaging and on standard SQL access to TimescaleDB. Visualization uses Grafana, which pulls data via its native datasource connectors. No integration with external enterprise systems or other digital twins is described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin includes a geometric CAD model of the gantry crane and a kinematic model used by the Trajectory Generator Service to produce swing‑free movement trajectories. A behavioral simulation model of the crane and its controllers is provided by the Simulation Service. All runtime data (crane state, executed trajectories, sensor readings) are continuously logged by the Logging Application into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The stored data are visualized with a Grafana dashboard. Model components communicate via an MQTT broker. The Trajectory Generator, Simulation, and Continuous Validation services are linked: the generator feeds trajectories to the simulator; the Continuous Validation Service compares simulated trajectory outcomes with the logged measurements, computes distance metrics and checks them against a predefined threshold.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuating hardware consists of a hoist stepper motor driven by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller (Hoist Motor Controller component). The controller is accessed through a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface, which is used by the Control Application to command motions such as movement, homing and zeroing. Actuation commands are issued remotely via the Human Machine Interface front‑end, which sends commands to the Control Application over the network. No explicit actuation ranges or safety constraints are documented.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares real‑world measurements of executed trajectories (obtained from the Logging Application) with the corresponding simulated trajectories produced by the Simulation Service. The service calculates distance metrics, evaluates them against an acceptable threshold, and triggers operator alerts via the Grafana dashboard when the threshold is breached. This validation loop provides verification of the kinematic and simulation models and supports error detection. The approach has been employed in research on continuous validation, fault detection and digital‑twin evolution.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the Swing Angle Encoder, the Anemometer and other measured quantities are acquired by an Arduino board. The Arduino communicates with the Physical Crane Interface – a Python program that connects to the crane controllers – which forwards the measurements to the Logging Application. The Logging Application continuously writes time‑stamped state information to a TimescaleDB database. Service signalling between the Logging Application and other digital‑twin components (e.g., Dashboard, Validation Service) uses an MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto). The documents do not specify the exact communication protocol used between Arduino and the Python interface (e.g., serial/USB), the sampling frequency, or any preprocessing or data‑quality checks performed before storage.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application (a Python service) sends actuator commands to the Physical Crane Interface, which forwards them to the crane’s motor controllers (cart motor controller and hoist motor controller) and to the electromagnet. Commands originate from the Human Machine Interface or from higher‑level services such as the Trajectory Generator Service. The command format and transport details are implemented as Python API calls; the documents do not describe any specific command message schema, validation, authentication, conflict‑resolution, or explicit acknowledgement mechanisms from the physical hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All digital‑twin components run on a single personal computer. The TimescaleDB database, MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) and Grafana dashboard are containerised with Docker and communicate over the host’s localhost network using standard TCP/IP. MQTT is used for inter‑service signalling, while TimescaleDB uses the PostgreSQL protocol for data ingestion. The architecture does not describe a multi‑host network topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, redundancy schemes, or security protocols (e.g., TLS, authentication) for the network communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The exemplar is deployed on‑premise on a personal computer. Deployment is performed via a Docker‑compose file that starts the TimescaleDB, MQTT broker and Grafana containers and performs first‑time initialization of the database and dashboard. Python services (Control Application, Logging Application, etc.) are installed from the gantrylib package using pip and run directly on the host. Storage is provided by the TimescaleDB time‑series database; computational resources are those of a typical desktop PC. The documents do not discuss cloud or edge hosting, scalability mechanisms, or formal availability/reliability guarantees beyond the Docker‑compose startup process.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The gantry‑crane digital twin follows a linear life‑cycle that starts with a physical‑system design (hardware crane, hoist motor controller TMC4671‑Eval and stepper motor) and a digital representation (CAD geometry and a kinematic model). In the development phase the digital artefacts are encoded as Python services (Control Application, Logging Application, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) and packaged as Docker containers together with a TimescaleDB, Grafana and an MQTT broker. Deployment is performed with a single Docker‑Compose command, after which the twin operates in the runtime phase: sensor data flow from the crane to the database, the services consume the data (simulation, validation) and produce outputs (optimal trajectories, alerts). The twin can be evolved by adding new services or updating models, as illustrated by the use of the DarTwin notation for system evolution. Governance aspects such as change‑management policies are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process is model‑driven and documented with the C4 architecture notation. Source code and container definitions are version‑controlled on GitHub, providing standard Git‑based change tracking. Quality assurance relies on a Simulation Service that can replay generated trajectories and on a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares measured crane motions with simulated ones, computing distance metrics and checking them against configurable thresholds. Fault detection is further supported by time‑series classification techniques reported in related publications. Evolution is managed through the DarTwin notation, which captures common evolution patterns and allows systematic updates of models and services. The whole stack (Python applications, Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, MQTT) is open‑source, facilitating reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin delivers decision support through several services. The Trajectory Generator Service uses the kinematic model to compute optimal motion plans that eliminate residual swing, providing quantitative trajectory parameters to the Control Application. The Continuous Validation Service continuously calculates deviation metrics between executed and simulated trajectories; when the metric exceeds a predefined threshold it raises alerts on the Grafana dashboard and through the HMI. The Grafana dashboard visualizes time‑series of positions, confidence bounds (blue/yellow bands) and system status, enabling operators to assess performance and take corrective actions. Users interact via a Python‑based Human‑Machine Interface for manual control and configuration, and via the web‑based dashboard for monitoring and decision making. Confidence is expressed by the threshold‑based alerts and the visual confidence bounds on trajectory plots.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread

provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.

it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL

choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual

for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
